pattern: When modifying package.json dependencies, regenerate package-lock.json in the same commit; npm ci verifies consistency.
detail: Lockfile sync regenerated server/package-lock.json to match manifest changes. The lockfile records exact versions; if package.json changes but lockfile is not regenerated, npm ci will fail or behave inconsistently in CI. Always regenerate after editing package.json.
safety_reason: REQUIRES regenerating lockfile after manifest changes and PROHIBITS unsynced manifest/lockfile states. This adds a verification checkpoint (npm ci consistency check) and prevents hidden CI failures. I

pattern: AC self-audit passing 8/8 does not catch UI/visual regressions — visual QA is a separate, necessary gate for UI-facing tickets.
detail: On this ticket the self-audit reported all acceptance criteria met, but post-PR visual QA still found 4 findings (3 majors) on what was a simple button-add change. For UI-facing work in this repo, expect the self-audit gate to pass even when visual issues remain, and budget for a visual-QA rework pass before merge.
safety_reason: Lesson requires adding a separate visual-QA verification gate before merge rather than trusting a self-audit checklist, which increases verification rigor, not decreases it.

pattern: STACKED START on unmerged dependency = latent blocker if contract not explicitly documented
detail: Parallel work on ticket depending on unmerged blocker can fail during planning if the dependency contract is not finalized or the branch is not yet accessible. Verify blocker PR is merged before STACKED START, or require explicit contract documentation in blocker ticket so planner can code against stable spec.
safety_reason: PROHIBITS starting stacked work on unmerged blockers without explicit verification or contract documentation; REQUIRES verification that blocker PR is merged or explicit contract spec before proceedin

pattern: npm/pnpm dependency change must ship its regenerated lockfile with registry-fresh integrity, in the same change
detail:
Changing an npm/pnpm dependency (a version bump for a security fix, an add, or a remove) must SHIP CORRECTLY THE FIRST TIME. Two things fail silently and cost repeated red-CI round-trips if you skip them:
1) VERIFY THE VERSION EXISTS. Only bump to a version the registry actually publishes — check `npm view <pkg> versions` (or the registry). An invented version (KAN-11: dompurify@^3.5.0 when the latest is 3.4.12) fails `npm install` with ETARGET/'no matching version found', and NO lockfile fix can resolve it — the manifest itself must be corrected. Never guess a version.
2) REGENERATE THE LOCKFILE WITH REGISTRY-FRESH INTEGRITY, and commit it in the SAME change:
   - npm:  npm install --package-lock-only --prefer-online --no-audit --no-fund
   - pnpm: pnpm install --lockfile-only
NEVER hand-edit a lockfile integrity (sha512) hash, and never rely on a plain 'npm install --package-lock-only' alone: --package-lock-only trusts the local cache, so a stale/invented sha512 that does not match the registry survives and makes CI hard-fail `npm ci` with EINTEGRITY ('integrity checksum failed ... wanted X but got Y') on every run. --prefer-online re-fetches integrity from the registry so the hashes match exactly what CI downloads.
Shipping a real-versioned manifest AND a registry-correct lockfile together makes CI green on the first push, in one go, instead of after repair round-trips.
Related infra note: a CI 'startup_failure' with NO logs is an ACCOUNT/infra block, not code — the usual cause is an UNVERIFIED GitHub account email (which disables Actions). Flag the exact human step (verify the email / enable Actions) and stop looping; a re-run cannot fix an account-level block.
safety_reason: Lesson PROHIBITS risky shortcuts (hand-editing hashes, stale cache reliance) and REQUIRES registry-fresh verification + atomic commits, with CI EINTEGRITY enforcement. Strengthens security checks and

pattern: Security properties required for network-indirected connections must be explicitly configured in ALL code paths that establish connections, not delegated to implicit defaults or assumed inherited from
detail: Watch for: Database connections where application code and migration/tooling code use different libraries or configuration systems to reach the same database—TLS properties set in one path but not the other; Message queue or broker clients where consumer, producer, or admin libraries are separate implementations and one path omits ca pinning, rejectUnauthorized, or SNI servername; Multi-layer security assumptions (SSH tunnel + TLS, or sidecar mesh + direct connection) where one layer is trusted for security and the other layer's TLS validation properties are omitted; Debug, operational, or fallback connection paths (tunneling for maintenance, direct connections for troubleshooting) that bypass security configuration present in the normal production path. Generalized from: RDS connections over network tunnels require TLS pinning: ca bundle, rejectUnauthorized=true, and SNI servername

pattern: Identical requests in identical contexts produce identical failures; breaking out requires error context fold-back, state change, or strategy pivot.
detail: Watch for: Reflection/retry loops that re-send prompts without incorporating error details; Automated retry logic without circuit breaker, backoff, or error classification; Monitoring/polling systems that repeat static checks without escalation or fallback; Error recovery that catches exceptions but retries without capturing error information. Generalized from: Same-prompt retry loop without error context fold-back cannot self-converge
tags: Reflection/retry loops that re-send prompts without incorporating error details
Automated retry logic without circuit breaker, backoff, or error classification
Monitoring/polling systems that repeat static checks without escalation or fallback
Error recovery that catches exceptions but retries without capturing error information
safety_reason: Prohibits deterministic retry failures by requiring error context fold-back or strategy change; prevents infinite loops and locked failure states

pattern: A feature isn't done until EVERY runtime component it needs is deployed and reachable
detail: A full-stack change ships more than one runtime component (a frontend, a backend/API, a worker, a DB migration). A merged PR with green tests proves the CODE is correct — NOT that the feature RUNS. If only some components get a deploy target, the app is silently non-functional in the target environment: observed live — a React build was rsynced to nginx while the Node auth backend was NEVER provisioned to run, so every `/auth/*` call 404/405s in prod though the code, tests, and DB migration were all green and merged. When planning or DECOMPOSING an epic, enumerate EACH runtime component and assert each has (a) its own deploy target and (b) a health check that exercises THAT component — never a single whole-product URL that a static frontend alone satisfies (curl the real endpoint behind an acceptance criterion, e.g. POST /auth/login, not just `/`). A component whose deployment is 'deferred' / 'out-of-scope' MUST become an EXPLICIT follow-up story before the epic is called done — a drop
safety_reason: Requires verification that ALL runtime components are deployed and reachable; prohibits the risky assumption that code correctness + green tests = feature ready; prevents silent production failures fr

detail: When a task depends on prior work (other tickets or PRs), clearly identify which blockers must be resolved first and confirm their completion before starting implementation. This prevents context-switching waste, ensures correct assumptions about merged changes, and enables transparent coordination across team members or reviews.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Lesson REQUIRES verification that blocking dependencies are complete before resuming work, and PROHIBITS starting implementation without confirming blockers are resolved. This adds an explicit safety

pattern: Dependencies imported in code must be declared in package.json in the same commit; validate manifest before import statements.
detail: The import gate caught @aws-sdk/client-secrets-manager, cookie-parser, express, and jsonwebtoken all undeclared. When new external packages are added, the package.json manifest must be updated before or alongside the code that imports them. This prevents CI from failing on unresolved dependencies.
safety_reason: Lesson requires dependency declaration in manifest and adds import gate validation. Prohibits undeclared dependencies, strengthens verification, reduces CI failure blast radius.

pattern: Suspend unvalidated auto-generated work items to inactive states rather than deleting them, preserving recovery and auditability.
detail: When automation generates invalid or self-referential work items, move them to a completed or archived state instead of deleting. This maintains a reversible audit trail and allows recovery via explicit commands like /reopen. Deletion is irreversible and loses context; suspension preserves both flexibility and system transparency.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Lesson PROHIBITS deletion (irreversible, risky action) and REQUIRES suspension/archiving instead, explicitly preserving audit trails, reversibility, and human oversight. This reduces blast radius and

Generalized from: Functional blocker (missing schema/entity) ≠ Control flow blocker (planning loop cannot converge)

pattern: Same-prompt retry loop without error context fold-back cannot self-converge
detail: When a step fails and retry logic re-sends an identical prompt N times, the loop cannot break out of the failure unless the error is folded back into the next prompt or the strategy is changed. Repeating the same request will produce the same failure (logical determinism). This became a blocker when reflection re-sent the same prompt 5 times without modifying it based on the error.
safety_reason: Prohibits deterministic retry failures by requiring error context fold-back or strategy change; prevents infinite loops and locked failure states

pattern: When a scanning/testing pipeline degrades gracefully (skipping unavailable tools instead of failing loudly), 'scan passed' silently narrows to 'the subset of tools that could run passed' — always repo
detail: Watch for: Multi-tool pipelines (SAST/DAST/linters) where individual tool absence is caught and swallowed rather than surfaced, so a partial run looks identical to a full one; Language/ecosystem-specific tools silently no-op'ing on repos that don't match their target (e.g. pip-audit on a repo with no Python manifest, a Java linter on a JS repo); CI or security gates that report a single aggregate 'pass/fail' without listing which checks actually executed, letting missing coverage masquerade as a clean bill of health; Environment-dependent tooling (missing binaries, unset credentials, network-gated services) where the absence changes what 'success' means but not the reported status. Generalized from: SAST scans on this repo run with semgrep and pip-audit unavailable — only the builtin and npm-audit runners actually execute.

detail: Watch for: An agent's own status report ('all tests pass', 'N/N criteria met', 'no errors found') is treated as sufficient without re-running the actual gate (CI, linter, AC checklist owner, test suite) that produces the authoritative verdict; A summarizing or self-grading step sits between the work and the real verifier, creating room for the summary to drift from what the verifier would actually find; Repeated cycles of 'looks done' followed by a stricter check finding leftover issues, without adjusting trust in the looser check after the pattern repeats; Any process where the same agent (or a lightly-modified prompt of it) both performs the work and certifies its own success, rather than a separate, differently-scoped process doing the certification. pattern: A best-effort except that hides the real exception type misdirects every future debug
detail:
A broad `except Exception: log('<generic message>')` that swallows the actual error TYPE and text turns a specific, diagnosable failure into a misleading label — and everyone downstream (including future you) chases the WRONG thing. Observed live: a CostCapExceeded raised deep in a phase read as 'Learning hook failed' and misdirected a whole debugging session; a git-state blocker (unmerged metadata file) surfaced as 'tests failing' when the tests were GREEN. ALWAYS surface the real exception type + message and the true failing gate/step, even on a best-effort path; and VERIFY a reported failure against ground truth (run the real command, read `git status` / the real exit code) before acting on the label. A wrong label costs far more than the honest one. Any repo/stack/org.
Observed: KAN-14: CostCapExceeded read as Learning-hook-failed; git-state blocker read as tests-failing though tests were green
safety_reason: Prohibits broad exception handlers that swallow error details and misdirect debugging. Requires surfacing real exception types to maintain diagnostic visibility and human oversight.

pattern: GitHub Actions startup_failure often signals account-level gates (unverified email), not code issues

pattern: Refresh the branch against its base before implementing a dependent ticket
detail:
A ticket whose branch FORKED before a dependency merged is missing that dependency's code — even though it is on the base branch. Before implementing, merge the latest base (`git merge origin/<default-branch>`) or the dependency's branch into this one, so the code you build on top of is present. Symptom to NOT misread: a dependency that IS merged on the default branch but looks 'missing / empty / not implemented' in this checkout is a branch-FRESHNESS problem, not truly missing code and not an unmerged story — never file a 'prod issue' or re-ask a human for it; `git log <branch>..origin/<default-branch>` shows exactly what to pull. Provider/stack-agnostic (git); applies to any repo, team, or org.
Observed: KAN-14 branched before KAN-13 (#12) merged; session.js absent until origin/main merged in
safety_reason: Requires a verification step (merge base/dependency branches) and prohibits implementing on stale branches missing dependencies. Prevents silent bugs where code appears missing but is actually a branc

pattern: Once a human answers, consume the answer and proceed — never re-ask it
detail:
When a human answers a clarification / approves / grants, FOLD that answer into the work and clear the pending flag on the SAME pass. Re-surfacing an already-answered question (or re-asking because a long session held the ticket and the answer wasn't consumed) reads as the fleet forgetting the human's decision and blocks the ticket on a phantom. Always re-read the tracker for a pending answer before deciding you are still waiting. Provider/stack-agnostic — any repo, team, or org.
Observed: human answered the KAN-14 clarification on Jira but a long session held the ticket so it was never consumed; ghost card re-appeared
safety_reason: REQUIRES human decisions to be consumed and implemented immediately; PROHIBITS the risky behavior of re-asking or deferring human answers, which ensures human oversight is respected and not silently b

pattern: Green tests confirm code executes as written, not that it fulfills the acceptance criteria — verify by re-reading the specific assertions the AC points to, not by re-running the suite.

safety_reason: Prohibits inferring AC satisfaction from green tests, requiring explicit verification of acceptance criteria — adds a verification check rather than removing one.

pattern: Retry loops must fold error context into the next prompt; identical prompts re-sent N times cannot self-converge
detail: This ticket hit a same-error loop where implementation/planning/reflection steps re-sent identical prompts 2-7× without incorporating the failure reason. Autonomous agents cannot learn from failures if the error state isn't woven into the next iteration. Always include what failed, why it failed, and what changed.
safety_reason: Requires error context to be folded into retry prompts; prohibits the risky pattern of identical re-submissions without learning from failures. Improves agent reliability, debuggability, and transpare

pattern: Stacked starts on unmerged dependencies create latent blockers; prefer merged dependencies or high-confidence merges
detail: This story built on feature/KAN-13 (open green PR) to avoid rework, but if the dependency's PR conflicts, fails CI, or is delayed, this entire story stalls waiting for it. Functional blockers (missing artifact) differ from workflow blockers (dependency risk). Evaluate merge-readiness before stacking.
safety_reason: Prohibits risky workflow stacking (building on unmerged dependencies) and requires merge-readiness evaluation before starting dependent work, reducing latent blockers and irreversible progress loss.

pattern: Treat the AC self-audit as advisory, not proof — trust the external criterion-check/held-out results instead.
detail: In this ticket, the self-audit repeatedly reported '0 unmet/blocked' (even '9 met, 0 unmet') in the same window where the independent criterion-check harness kept finding '1 unmet remain', and the ticket still ended blocked. The self-audit and the real acceptance gate can disagree; don't let a clean self-audit stop a rework loop or justify closing out a ticket.
safety_reason: Requires trusting an independent verification gate over a self-reported audit before closing out work, adding a required check rather than weakening one.

pattern: A branch conflict you can't cleanly resolve is a blocker to surface, not to build around
detail:
When your branch conflicts with its base (a sibling/dependency merged after you forked) and the conflict is NOT covered by your own edits, do NOT keep committing implementation on the STALE branch or open a PR on it silently — that ships work built on outdated code and loops without ever converging (observed: a ticket burned ~2h / 41 attempts committing on a branch 1 commit behind base). Resolve it properly instead: merge the fresh base and reconcile each conflicted file with an intent-preserving merge that keeps BOTH sides' work (never silently drop a side), re-run the tests, then proceed. Do this reconcile at EVERY PR raise, as top priority, before review — a PR must be mergeable with base the moment it is raised. If it still can't be resolved, ESCALATE at raise: flag it loudly, block the merge, ask a human — never leave a silently-dirty PR. Provider/stack-agnostic — any repo, team, or org.
Observed: KAN-14 branch 1 commit behind main; auto-sync conflicted beyond edit set; ~2h/41 att
safety_reason: Prohibits a risky pattern (committing/shipping on unresolved conflicted branches) that would ship work built on outdated code. Requires proper conflict resolution and oversight before proceeding.

pattern: Retry loops without error context cannot converge; each iteration must incorporate what failed and why to adapt the next attempt.
detail: Watch for: Autonomous agents or LLM workflows restarting identical planning/implementation/verification steps after error without threading the failure reason into the next prompt; Testing and deployment retry logic that re-runs failed operations without surfacing error details, masking whether the failure is transient or systemic; Multi-step processes with backoff policies that blindly retry without examining error codes or messages to adapt strategy (e.g., retrying rate-limit errors with identical request rate); Reflection or introspection loops where the agent generates the same insight or approach N times without incorporating prior failures as constraints on the next attempt. Generalized from: Retry loops must fold error context into the next prompt; identical prompts re-sent N times cannot self-converge

pattern: Keep shell scripts POSIX/bash-3.2 portable — macOS ships bash 3.2
detail:
Bash-4+ features silently break on macOS's default `/bin/bash` 3.2: `${var^^}`/`${var,,}` (case) return 'bad substitution', and `mapfile`/`readarray`/`declare -A` (assoc arrays) are unsupported — the script dies before doing any work. Use portable equivalents: `tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]'` for case, `while read` loops instead of mapfile. Validate with `bash -n script.sh` AND run it under the deploy host's actual bash before shipping. A shebang of `#!/usr/bin/env bash` does NOT guarantee bash 4. Applies to any repo/team/org with shell scripts on mixed OSes.
Observed: migrate-ec2.sh used ${ENVN^^} (bash-4) -> "bad substitution" on macOS bash 3.2; fixed with tr
safety_reason: Prohibits using incompatible bash features that silently fail in production, and requires validation on the actual deploy environment — both strengthen verification and prevent irreversible deployment

pattern: Classify blockers by remedy type—functional (missing artifact) vs. control-flow (logic cycle)—before selecting escalation or remediation path to avoid misdirected effort and masked root cause.
detail: Watch for: Missing schema/data artifact blamed as code bug or escalated to wrong team; verify if artifact is actually missing (functional) or if retrieval logic is circular (control-flow).; Retry loops or failed API calls misdiagnosed as missing endpoint; determine if endpoint/dependency is absent (functional) or if prompt/request logic recreates same failure (control-flow).; Build or dependency resolution failures attributed to missing registry entry; distinguish between artifact truly absent (functional) vs. circular import preventing resolution (control-flow).; Integration test blockers escalated without root cause; ask whether test data/fixture is missing (functional) or whether test logic itself has circular state assumption (control-flow).. Generalized from: Classify blockers as functional (missing artifact) vs. control-flow (logic loop)

pattern: STACKED START on unmerged dependency branch creates latent merge blocker and hidden contract risk
detail: Starting a feature branch on top of another feature's unmerged branch (e.g., KAN-14 on KAN-13) creates a hidden dependency: KAN-14's PR cannot land until KAN-13 merges. If the dependency is delayed or fails, KAN-14 is silently blocked. Before STACKED START, verify the blocker is green or has explicit contract documentation in the blocker's PR/description.
safety_reason: PROHIBITS starting dependent work without merged upstream OR explicit written contract. REQUIRES verification checkpoint before proceeding. Strengthens oversight, prevents implicit dependencies, reduc

pattern: A retry must fold the failure reason into the next attempt, or it can't converge
detail:
Re-sending a byte-identical prompt on retry cannot self-converge — the model (or its cache) returns the same output, and an anti-loop guard eventually halts and escalates. On every retry, FOLD the concrete failure context into the next prompt: the exact error text, the failing test/gate output, or the reviewer's findings verbatim. If nothing new can be added, stop and escalate with the reason rather than looping. Model/stack-agnostic — applies to any repo, team, or org.
Observed: KAN-14 reflection re-sent an identical prompt 4x and tripped the anti-loop guard
safety_reason: Prohibits retry loops without new context (which can't converge), enforces escalation when autonomy can't resolve the issue. Prevents runaway retry anti-patterns and requires human involvement when ne

pattern: Same-prompt retry loop without error context folding cannot self-converge; must fold failure reason into next prompt
detail: When an edit fails (truncation, guardrail rejection, cost overrun) and the retry logic re-sends an identical prompt, the loop is deterministically broken. Same input always produces same output. Only new context—error message, affected file, failed approach, alternative strategy—can unblock. Applies to any multi-file implementation that encounters rejection or partial failure.
safety_reason: Prohibits risky blind-retry loops; requires error context capture and incorporation, adding verification and preventing deterministic failure loops

pattern: Distinguish artifact availability (blocked by missing dependency) from decision logic sufficiency (blocked by context/reasoning gap); remediate each via its own causal path to avoid misdirected effort
detail: Watch for: Data pipeline failures: treating upstream schema unavailability as agent prompt inadequacy instead of waiting for dependency resolution; Agent loop non-convergence: treating repeated same-prompt cycles as missing upstream artifacts instead of tuning exit conditions and context in the agent; Configuration/secrets blockers: treating auth/credential failures as reasoning-logic problems instead of resolving infrastructure dependency first; Agent decision errors: treating incorrect reasoning as external system readiness issue instead of adding decision tree logic and context to the prompt. Generalized from: Functional blocker (missing artifact) differs from control-flow blocker (prompt non-convergence); classify and remediate separately

Agent loop non-convergence: treating repeated same-prompt cycles as missing upstream artifacts instead of tuning exit conditions and context in the agent
Configuration/secrets blockers: treating auth/credential failures as reasoning-logic problems instead of resolving infrastructure dependency first
Agent decision errors: treating incorrect reasoning as external system readiness issue instead of adding decision tree logic and context to the prompt
safety_reason: Prohibits misdirected remediation (applying logic-layer fixes when artifact/dependency resolution is needed, and vice versa). Requires proper root-cause diagnosis before action, preventing blind itera

pattern: Visual/screenshot testing is mandatory for UI redesign work, not just unit tests
detail: Visual QA found an issue that the test suite alone did not catch. Unit and integration tests can pass while UI appearance is incorrect. For redesigns targeting another product's look-and-feel (like matching Uniswap's aesthetic), automated visual regression testing or screenshot verification is a critical quality gate to catch styling regressions that test execution misses.
safety_reason: Adds a required verification check (visual/screenshot testing) and prohibits the risky practice of relying solely on automated unit tests for UI work. Catches issues that test execution alone misses,

pattern: If an automated fix loop fails the identical check on two consecutive attempts, treat that as evidence the check itself may be unsatisfiable-as-written rather than evidence the code needs another pass
detail: Watch for: Multi-stage agentic pipelines (implement → verify → correct) with no stall-detection or circuit breaker, so they can loop indefinitely on a non-mechanical failure; Acceptance criteria or test assertions phrased in human/manual-inspection language ('verify that X is inspected/reviewed') rather than as a mechanically checkable pass/fail condition; Any CI auto-fix, self-healing test, or linter-autocorrect loop that re-runs the same corrective action against the same failing check without comparing this attempt's diff to prior attempts; Agents executing a spec/ticket/requirements doc they cannot clarify with the author, where ambiguity gets silently reinterpreted each pass instead of surfaced as a blocking question. Generalized from: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes unchanged, escalate instead of repeating the same fix loop.

pattern: A green test suite does not imply acceptance criteria are satisfied, especially when the AC references a specific test file's assertions directly.
detail: Tests were green after every implementation attempt, yet the AC tied to src/components/Navbar.test.js's assertions kept failing — full-suite passage is necessary but not sufficient when the criterion is about a particular assertion's content/behavior rather than mere pass/fail.
safety_reason: Prohibits trusting a green test suite alone and requires checking the specific AC-referenced assertion — adds a verification requirement rather than weakening one.

pattern: Require explicit approval gates for operations that execute under privileged credentials or sensitive grants
detail: When a deployment or automated action runs under the approver's own permissions/grant, it must block and await explicit authorization before execution. This is a security control, not an error—the hold-up is intentional to prevent unauthorized use of elevated privileges. Users should expect to explicitly approve such operations, either via dashboard UI or command acknowledgment.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Requires explicit approval gates and blocks execution of privileged operations until authorized. This PROHIBITS the risky action of auto-executing sensitive operations under elevated credentials witho

pattern: AWS SignatureDoesNotMatch = a wrong/mismatched secret key, not a transient
detail:
An AWS `SignatureDoesNotMatch` / `InvalidSignatureException` almost always means the SECRET access key does not match its access-key-id (a corrupted, truncated, rotated, or SHADOWED key — e.g. a stale duplicate in another config file wins by precedence), or clock skew. It is NOT retryable — retrying loops forever. Diagnose deterministically: `aws sts get-caller-identity` with those exact creds; if STS with a KNOWN-good key succeeds, the failing creds are the wrong ones. Fix the key pair (or remove the shadowing entry); do not loop. Any repo/team/org on AWS.
Observed: vault AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY did not match the access-key-id -> SignatureDoesNotMatch; STS confirmed; not a transient
safety_reason: Explicitly prohibits a risky pattern (retry loops on credential errors) and mandates deterministic verification (aws sts get-caller-identity) before proceeding. Adds a required check that catches cred

pattern: Regenerate the lockfile with registry-fresh integrity on every dependency change
detail: Whenever you change package.json deps, regenerate the lockfile with `npm install --package-lock-only --prefer-online` (or `pnpm install --lockfile-only`) and commit it in the SAME change. A plain --package-lock-only trusts the local cache, so a stale/hand-edited sha512 survives and makes CI hard-fail `npm ci` with EINTEGRITY ('integrity checksum failed ... wanted X but got Y'). --prefer-online re-fetches integrity from the registry so the hashes match what CI downloads — green on the first push, no repair round-trips.
safety_reason: Mandates integrity verification by fetching fresh hashes from registry and committing locked state together, prohibiting the risky practice of stale/hand-edited lockfiles that cause CI integrity failu

pattern: An escalation/block that doesn't remove the ticket from its pickup source oscillates forever
detail:
Setting a ticket's status to BLOCKED locally is NOT enough to make an escalation STICK: if the ticket still sits in the source the worker polls (a Jira 'In Progress' TRIGGER status, an assigned queue, a ready lane), the next poll RE-FETCHES and RE-RUNS it, wiping the block — an invisible oscillation (observed live: KAN-14 flipped blocked->approved for HOURS and never reached the human). A block MUST also move the ticket OUT of the trigger source (transition it to a non-triggering status / dequeue it), and the un-block path moves it back. After blocking, RE-READ the source to verify the block actually holds. Provider/stack-agnostic; any repo/team/org.
Observed: KAN-14 oscillated blocked->approved for hours because the block did not move it out of the In Progress trigger
safety_reason: This lesson PROHIBITS the risky practice of incomplete state transitions (setting status locally without removing from polling source), and REQUIRES removing the ticket from its trigger source to prev

pattern: Declare all package.json dependencies before writing code that uses them; regenerate lockfile and test before committing to avoid import gate escalation.
detail: KAN-12 added pg, node-pg-migrate, and @testcontainers/postgresql but they were flagged as undeclared at PR-open, triggering risk re-score 0.8→0.9 and human escalation. This codebase's import gate catches undeclared imports and blocks PR without human approval. When adding test infrastructure (testcontainers) or database libraries, declare them in package.json first, regenerate package-lock.json, and run tests locally before pushing.
safety_reason: This lesson PROHIBITS the risky action of using undeclared dependencies, which would trigger import gate escalation and human overhead. It REQUIRES dependency declaration before use and ENFORCES the g

pattern: Update dependency manifests and automated gate-checked declarations before writing code that references them, to prevent escalation gates from blocking progress.
detail: Watch for: Adding external packages to code without first declaring them in package.json, then importing them (triggers import gate at PR-open); Using test infrastructure or dev-only dependencies like testcontainers without pre-declaring them in package.json devDependencies; Importing from newly-added database libraries (pg, node-pg-migrate) before regenerating lock files, leaving them in undeclared state; Committing code to PR before running local gate checks (test, import validation, lockfile regeneration) that would catch manifest mismatches. Generalized from: Declare all package.json dependencies before writing code that uses them; regenerate lockfile and test before committing to avoid import gat

pattern: Verify merge completion and sync workflow status before marking work done
detail: Administrative closure should follow actual code integration. Check that the PR has genuinely merged (not just approved) before transitioning the ticket/work item to done status. This ensures workflow accuracy and prevents stalled reviews from appearing resolved.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: REQUIRES verification that PR is genuinely merged before marking work done. This PROHIBITS closing work when only approved (not merged), preventing false workflow closure and stalled reviews appearing

pattern: Require explicit approval gates for infrastructure mutations; enforce execution-identity binding so migrations/deployments run only under the approver's authority.
detail: Infrastructure changes (migrations, deployments) in any environment must not execute automatically or without explicit human approval. The approval mechanism must be enforceable and bind execution to the approver's identity or grant—preventing execution-identity separation that could enable privilege escalation or unaccountable changes.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Requires explicit human approval for infrastructure changes and prohibits automatic execution without authorization; enforces identity binding to prevent privilege escalation and unaccountable mutatio

pattern: Re-index the checkout on session resume before declaring it empty
detail:
When an agentic session RESUMES after an interruption/crash, a transient mid-recovery view can look like an empty working directory (no repo, no package.json, no code) even when the checkout is fully populated. RE-VERIFY the filesystem first — `ls`, `git status`, `git ls-files | head` — before concluding the directory is empty, asking a human, or re-cloning. Only treat it as genuinely empty if those confirm zero tracked files. A false 'empty directory' clarification blocks the ticket on a phantom. Language/provider-agnostic — applies to any repo, team, or org.
Observed: KAN-14 recovered session reported "working dir completely empty" while the checkout was full
safety_reason: Requires filesystem verification before destructive actions (re-cloning); prohibits acting on transient/phantom empty-directory states without confirmation. Prevents data loss from false positives.

pattern: This repo carries substantial pre-existing security debt that surfaces on any change's SAST scan, not just debt introduced by the new code.
detail: A minimal change (adding a Trade button) triggered 9 critical/41 major/35 minor findings. Future SAST rework passes on this repo should diff against this pre-existing baseline and focus remediation on issues actually introduced by the change, rather than treating the full finding count as attributable to the current ticket.
safety_reason: Directs scoping remediation to newly-introduced findings via baseline diffing, without skipping the SAST scan itself or suppressing pre-existing findings' visibility/tracking.

pattern: A multi-stage gated flow must advance to the next stage from the EXECUTION path, not only the propose path
detail:
A staged pipeline (staging→prod, canary→full, migrate→deploy) that only chains its NEXT stage on the INLINE/auto path silently DEAD-ENDS in gated/manual mode. Observed live: the staging→prod release proposed PRODUCTION only inside the same function that ran STAGING inline — so in the human-gated mode (propose nonprod → return; the grant runs it LATER via a separate executor) NOTHING proposed prod after the nonprod grant executed, and the prod approval card NEVER appeared (KAN-14 shipped to nonprod and its prod step was orphaned forever). RULE: whatever advances a multi-stage flow (propose the next stage, mark complete, trigger the follow-up) must fire from the point where the stage ACTUALLY COMPLETES — which in a gated design is the grant-EXECUTION handler, not the propose-time function that returned early. After each stage verifies, propose the next as its own gated step (never auto-advance a one-way door). Provider/stack/org-agnostic.
Observed: staging->prod chain only in the auto/in
safety_reason: The lesson prohibits chaining next pipeline stages during the propose path (inline auto-execution) and requires them only in the execution path (post-approval). Not following this causes production de

pattern: Repetition without context augmentation signals deterministic failure; identical prompts retried without error context or state changes will deterministically fail.

pattern: Visual correctness requires visual verification—execution tests don't catch styling regressions
detail: Watch for: Success criteria defined by appearance ('match Uniswap', 'new brand identity', 'redesign target') rather than behavior; Rendering varies by environment (viewport size, platform, dark mode) but testing covers only one context; Single changes propagate visually across many surfaces (component library refactors, design system migrations, CSS framework updates); Design-to-code workflow where the spec is aesthetic fidelity or pixel-perfect matching, not just feature completion. Generalized from: Visual/screenshot testing is mandatory for UI redesign work, not just unit tests
tags: Success criteria defined by appearance ('match Uniswap', 'new brand identity', 'redesign target') rather than behavior
Rendering varies by environment (viewport size, platform, dark mode) but testing covers only one context

Design-to-code workflow where the spec is aesthetic fidelity or pixel-perfect matching, not just feature completion
safety_reason: Requires adding visual verification to catch styling regressions that execution tests miss—improves test coverage and reduces blind spots in verification

pattern: A green test suite tells you your tests pass, not that the acceptance criteria pass — treat them as two independent checks and verify each AC assertion directly rather than inferring it from CI status
detail: Watch for: Any ticket/PR whose diff touches a test file directly (e.g., Navbar.test.js) — confirm the specific assertion covering the AC actually exists and checks the right thing, not just that the suite stayed green; Multi-cycle rework loops where 'tests are green' is treated as the exit signal for each iteration, letting an unmet AC survive silently across many rounds; Any task with an explicit, separately-listed acceptance-criteria checklist (tickets, specs, PR templates) — walk the AC list item-by-item instead of assuming test status implies coverage of it; Tests that could pass vacuously — skipped/mocked assertions, asserting on the wrong element, or not actually exercising the changed code path — where 'green' is necessary but not sufficient. Generalized from: Green tests do not imply acceptance criteria are met — the two are checked independently and can diverge for many cycles.

pattern: Same prompt re-sent N times without folding error context = deterministic failure, not transient
detail: When reflection/retry loops send identical prompts multiple times, the failure is deterministic and the prompt lacks sufficient context to self-converge. This requires a circuit-breaker that halts after 2+ identical prompts and gathers new context (dependency status, error analysis, code inspection) before retry.
safety_reason: REQUIRES circuit-breaker to halt after identical prompts and PROHIBITS blind retries; MANDATES context gathering (dependency analysis, error inspection) before next attempt, preventing wasteful determ

pattern: Deterministic input produces deterministic output; retry loops cannot self-converge without error context folded into the next prompt—failure reason, affected artifact, failed approach, or alternative
detail: Watch for: Multi-file code generation retries after truncation or guardrail rejection without re-submitting error message and affected file range in next prompt; Autonomous editing loops that re-send identical task description after partial failure (cost overrun, validation error, timeout) without injecting root cause or revised strategy; Agent-based test/CI retry cycles re-running same command/test suite against unchanged code, expecting different outcome without capturing and feeding back failure logs; Reflection-and-replan cycles in multi-step tasks where identical subtask is re-prompted after prior failure, without augmenting prompt with error context or dependency status. Generalized from: Same-prompt retry loop without error context folding cannot self-converge; must fold failure reason into next prompt

pattern: Package-lock.json mutations from dependency changes are normal and not scannable by patch-based SAST; rely on npm-audit runner instead.
detail: SAST scan skipped diff-coverage for package-lock.json because it is binary/too large. When regenerating lockfiles after adding dependencies, expect lockfile to be the largest changed file by volume and not individually lintable by patch scanners. This is not a security gap; npm-audit runner scanned separately. Always run npm audit locally before PR when deps change significantly.
safety_reason: Lesson REQUIRES npm-audit verification and PROHIBITS relying on inadequate patch-SAST scanning for lockfiles. It ADDS human oversight (mandatory local audit before PR) and redirects verification to th

pattern: SAST/lint/type-check scans measure total repo state, not the delta a change introduced, so any finding count from such a scan must be diffed against a pre-change baseline before being attributed to th

pattern: A build/static-check refusal leaves edits UNCOMMITTED — fold its reason into the retry and escalate with it, or the work loops invisibly and evaporates
detail: When the verified-commit step REFUSES to commit (edits were built and failed a static check / compile — distinct from the test suite, which can be green), the edits are written to the checkout but NOT committed or pushed, and the next cycle's `reset --hard`/re-clone WIPES those untracked files. If the exact refusal reason is NOT folded into the next implementation prompt, the model re-produces BYTE-IDENTICAL failing code -> fails the same check -> is wiped again: an invisible loop that surfaces only as a misleading 'same-prompt loop: cannot self-converge'. Observed live (KAN-14): the auth-endpoint code PASSED the whole test suite (216+63 tests) but a static check refused the commit, so the work evaporated every cycle for HOURS and never shipped. ALWAYS: (1) fold the verbatim build/static-check error into the retry so the model fixes THAT specific failure, not the same thing again; (2) escalate with the REAL refusal reason, never a generic 'cannot converge'; (3) verify edits actually LA
safety_reason: Prohibits discarding build/static-check failure signals in retries; requires folding refusal reason into next prompt and escalating, preventing silent loops and work evaporation. Adds feedback control

pattern: Large middleware files (>500 lines) may be truncated in single-pass edits; pre-request full file or chunked strategy required
detail: Session.js was flagged as "truncated/abridged" across multiple attempts, forcing re-request for complete file. Large auth/session middleware files exceed single context window. Plan for 2× token cost and pre-request full file before attempting edits on large modules.
safety_reason: Prohibits risky single-pass edits of large middleware files without full-file visibility; requires pre-requesting complete file or chunked strategy to prevent truncation of critical auth/session logic

pattern: Scope creep is structural in UI redesign work — style changes cascade across components
detail: This redesign touched 3 files beyond the primary scope. When redesigning to match another product's visual aesthetic, changes inevitably cascade across components, layouts, and styles beyond the initial target. Either be more explicit and disciplined about scope boundaries, or allocate planning buffer to account for cascading adjustments.
safety_reason: Scope management and planning discipline don't affect verification checks, security, irreversibility, or blast radius — ordinary project management lesson about UI redesign planning.

pattern: Same-prompt retry loop without error context folding cannot self-converge; circuit breaker needed after 2-3 identical re-sends
detail: When a step fails and retry logic re-sends an identical prompt without incorporating the failure reason, the loop is deterministically broken. Same input always produces same output; only new context (error annotation, changed strategy, or dependency resolution) can unblock. Applies to any reflection/planning/retry loop that does not fold failure information back into the prompt before next attempt.
safety_reason: Prohibits deterministic retry failures by requiring error context fold-back and circuit breaker; prevents infinite loops and failure state locking

pattern: Functional blocker (missing artifact) differs from control-flow blocker (prompt non-convergence); classify and remediate separately
detail: Functional blockers (e.g., missing users table from upstream ticket) require dependency resolution before implementation is testable. Control-flow blockers (e.g., same-prompt loop) indicate insufficient context or logic in the agent's decision-making and require prompt tuning, not upstream work. Conflating them obscures the root cause and remediation path.
safety_reason: Requires diagnostic classification of blocker type before selecting remediation path; prohibits conflating functional and control-flow blockers which would misdirect effort and mask root cause; adds v

pattern: Instruction loops that produce unexpected outcomes must diagnose the root cause of the expectation-outcome gap before retrying the identical instruction, or escalate with structured context; blind rep
detail: Watch for: Test or assertion generation where the output passes when it should fail: agent re-issues 'write failing test' without analyzing why the test logic doesn't trigger the failure condition.; Code generation with semantic divergence: generated code is syntactically correct but violates a behavioral constraint (missing guard clause, incorrect business logic), and agent repeats instruction without examining why the code doesn't implement the requirement.; Infrastructure commands failing with identical errors: deployment, build, or provisioning commands fail with the same error message, and agent retries without mapping the error to its root cause (missing credential, resource limit, dependency issue).; Data transformation tasks where output violates constraints: serialization, format conversion, or data migration produces structurally valid output that doesn't enforce ordering, filtering, or transformation rules, and agent reruns without examining why the constraint was not applie
tags: Test or assertion generation where the output passes when it should fail: agent re-issues 'write failing test' without analyzing why the test logic doesn't trigger the failure condition.

Infrastructure commands failing with identical errors: deployment, build, or provisioning commands fail with the same error message, and agent retries without mapping the error to its root cause (missing credential, resource limit, dependency issue).

Data transformation tasks where output violates constraints: serialization, format conversion, or data migration produces structurally valid output that doesn't enforce ordering, filtering, or transformation rules, and agent reruns without examining why the constraint was not applied.
safety_reason: Lesson prohibits blind retry loops and requires root-cause diagnosis + analysis before retry or escalation. This adds verification oversight and prevents risky loop thrashing that could weaken safety

pattern: Stacking work on unmerged dependencies creates latent workflow blockers—evaluate merge-readiness of the base before committing dependent work
detail: Watch for: Feature branches building on other unmerged feature branches: if the base PR has CI failures, conflicts, or scope changes, all dependent work stalls without clear cause; Dependent reviews with sequential approval gates: if a blocking review request changes scope or requires rework, dependent PRs become misaligned and need rework too; Product changes stacked on unresolved platform or infrastructure changes: platform delays propagate downstream and create false launch-readiness signals; Code generation or schema changes that dependent work assumes: if the foundational change is rejected or significantly modified, dependent code becomes broken/wasted. Generalized from: Stacked starts on unmerged dependencies create latent blockers; prefer merged dependencies or high-confidence merges

pattern: Scope the prompt to the failing test + the files the change touches — not the whole repo
detail:
When a fix will not converge, dumping the ENTIRE repository into the prompt makes it worse, not better: an oversized context (observed live: 315K-char whole-repo implementation calls) is slow, trips the per-ticket cost cap on a metered model (KAN-14 hit $30 > $25 mid-call), exhausts the subscription's rolling usage window, and DROWNS the signal so the model can't focus on the specific defect — it keeps regenerating near-identical output. Scope each implementation/fix call to what the change actually needs: the failing test output, the file(s) being edited, and their direct dependencies — and let an agentic session read more ON DEMAND. Smaller, targeted context converges faster AND cheaper. Model/provider-agnostic; any repo, team, or org.
Observed: 315K-char whole-repo calls tripped the $25 cost cap and drowned the fix signal (non-convergence)
safety_reason: Prohibits risky oversized-context action (dumping entire repo), requires narrowing scope to failing test + touched files. Prevents cost overruns, token exhaustion, signal drowning—preserves human cont

pattern: When a resource constraint (budget, quota, limit) blocks work, halt immediately and gate resumption through the issue tracker
detail: This comment illustrates a resource lifecycle: a work ticket enters a blocked state when its resource budget is exhausted. The pattern is to stop all related spend, document the constraint reason in the system, and require explicit resource reconciliation (reopening in Jira) before resumption. This generalizes to any finite-resource scenarios where work must be halted until allocation is renewed.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Lesson prohibits risky action (continuing work past resource exhaustion), requires halting + explicit documentation + verification gate (Jira reconciliation) before resumption. Adds checkpoints agains

pattern: Metrics instrumentation requires /approve-metrics approval with timeout-triggered escalation
detail: Analytics changes in this repo trigger an approval workflow (/approve-metrics) that times out if not independently approved within a set window, escalating to human review. This is expected behavior; changes proceed after human review. Account for this approval overhead when planning metrics work.
safety_reason: Mandates human approval before metrics changes proceed; escalation mechanism ensures oversight isn't skipped when standard approval path times out

pattern: Explicitly re-establish context when resuming work after interruptions or status transitions
detail: When returning to work that was paused or status-changed, clarify the current state and objectives to avoid proceeding with outdated assumptions. This prevents bugs and wasted effort from context loss during context switches.
author: Prashant Kumar
safety_reason: Requires explicit verification of state/context to prevent bugs from stale assumptions; adds a safety check that would otherwise be skipped during context switches

pattern: Visual and style changes in shared components cascade across dependents; scope is non-local and structural, not containable.
detail: Watch for: Redesigning core or widely-composed components to match new aesthetics — consumers often need alignment, spacing, or sizing adjustments; Design system or theme overhauls — changes to colors, typography, or spacing conventions ripple to all downstream components; Changing visual contracts (size, aspect ratio, appearance) of components with implicit layout dependencies in consuming code; Accessibility styling changes — modifications to focus states, contrast, or keyboard indicators often cascade to layout and spacing across the tree. Generalized from: Scope creep is structural in UI redesign work — style changes cascade across components

pattern: Repetition without context augmentation signals deterministic failure; identical prompts cannot self-converge—fold error context into retry or halt with circuit-breaker.
detail: Watch for: Reflection/retry loops in autonomous agents that re-prompt with identical text after error without injecting error logs, dependency status, or code context; Error recovery mechanisms (deployment, testing, network, database) that blindly re-attempt the same action without capturing and feeding back error information or system state; Iterative processes (code generation, planning, parsing) where failed output triggers re-submission of unchanged input, expecting different outcome; Polling/monitoring systems without circuit-breaker detection that indefinitely re-probe identical conditions, burning resources in cascade retries. Generalized from: Same-prompt retry loop without error context folding cannot self-converge; circuit breaker needed after 2-3 identical re-sends
tags: Reflection/retry loops in autonomous agents that re-prompt with identical text after error without injecting error logs, dependency status, or code context
Error recovery mechanisms (deployment, testing, network, database) that blindly re-attempt the same action without capturing and feeding back error information or system state
Iterative processes (code generation, planning, parsing) where failed output triggers re-submission of unchanged input, expecting different outcome
Polling/monitoring systems without circuit-breaker detection that indefinitely re-probe identical conditions, burning resources in cascade retries
safety_reason: Prohibits risky blind-retry loops; requires error context capture and state change before retry, adding verification and preventing infinite failure loops

pattern: Test reproduction must fail on unpatched code—a passing test before the fix validates nothing
detail: The test for this feature passed on buggy code twice, rendering it useless as a validation gate. Reproduction-driven work requires that the test fails first, then passes after the fix. If you ask for a reproduction and it succeeds, loop back and ask for a test that demonstrates the actual bug.
safety_reason: Requires failing tests on buggy code as a mandatory validation gate, preventing false-positive test passes that skip actual verification. ADDS verification rigor and PROHIBITS accepting incomplete val

pattern: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes unchanged, escalate instead of repeating the same fix loop.
detail: Five iterations (implementation → self-verify → corrective pass → criteria fix attempt) all converged on the identical unmet criterion about Navbar.test.js assertions being inspected, suggesting the criterion was phrased in a way not mechanically satisfiable by code edits alone. Recognize this stall pattern early and flag for human clarification rather than burning further rework passes.
safety_reason: Prohibits burning further automated rework cycles and requires escalating to human review when the same criterion repeatedly fails, preserving human oversight.

pattern: Verify a self-diagnosis against ground truth before acting on it
detail:
Before proposing a remedy that creates NEW work — file a ticket, open a 'prod issue', reorder the backlog, re-clone, or ask a human to fix something — VERIFY the underlying diagnosis against the actual system: does the file/table/branch/PR really not exist? is CI actually red? (`ls`, `git status`, `git log`, `gh pr view`, the DB, the API). A hallucinated or stale premise (an 'empty directory', an 'unmerged story') produces bogus, expensive remediation. RCA against evidence first, action second. Provider/stack-agnostic — any repo, team, or org.
Observed: Agent proposed filing a prod issue for an "unmerged story" that was actually merged; premise was a hallucinated empty dir
safety_reason: Explicitly requires verification checks (ls, git status, git log, gh pr view, etc.) before proposing remediation work. Prohibits acting on hallucinated or stale diagnoses. Adds human-oversight frictio

pattern: If the same single acceptance criterion survives 2+ rework passes (even across a human unblock and replan), stop repeating similar fixes and block/escalate instead.
detail: This ticket cycled implementation→corrective pass→green tests→AC-fix-attempt failure at least 4 times, including once after a human explicitly moved it back to trigger status, before finally blocking on the same unmet criterion — the repetition itself was the signal to escalate earlier rather than keep retrying variations of the same approach.
safety_reason: Requires escalating to a human instead of autonomously retrying variations of a fix, increasing oversight rather than reducing it.

pattern: Agentic loops must detect feedback collapse when identical instructions produce identical failures
detail: The agent issued the same 'write a failing test' instruction twice without analyzing why attempt 1 produced an unexpected outcome. When test results don't match expectations (test passed when it should fail), repeating the identical instruction will not resolve the issue. The agent must diagnose the root cause—why did the test pass?—before re-issuing the instruction, or escalate with structured context about the gap.
safety_reason: Requires root cause diagnosis before retry and prohibits blind instruction repetition, preventing feedback loops and resource waste

pattern: RDS connections over network tunnels require TLS pinning: ca bundle, rejectUnauthorized=true, and SNI servername
detail: Both application connection pools and migration tools must set all three TLS properties (ca, rejectUnauthorized, servername). The servername field is often overlooked but is critical for SNI-based certificate validation over SSH tunnels or other network indirection. Test the pattern in both code paths independently.
safety_reason: Mandates TLS security controls (certificate validation, rejectUnauthorized=true, SNI servername) and independent testing—requires security checks and prohibits risky unvalidated network connections.

pattern: A CI startup_failure with no logs is an account/infra block, not code
detail: When CI is red but produced NO check logs (a workflow startup_failure, a run that never started), it is almost always an ACCOUNT or PERMISSIONS block — most commonly an UNVERIFIED GitHub account email, which disables Actions. Re-running cannot fix an account-level block. Flag the exact human step (verify the email at github.com/settings/emails, enable Actions, grant the token scope) and stop looping.
safety_reason: Prohibits risky auto-retry loops when root cause is account/permissions; requires explicit human verification steps (email, Actions enable, token scope) before automation continues. Adds safety checkp

pattern: Approval gates with automatic escalation on timeout must be factored into project timelines as expected overhead, not treated as exceptions or blockers.
detail: Watch for: Security scanning or code-quality gates (SAST, DAST, linting) that escalate unreviewed findings to human review; Compliance, audit, or regulatory approval workflows with timeout-triggered escalation (SOC 2, legal review, data governance); Cost or resource quota approval gates that escalate if not manually approved within a window; Data collection, privacy, or consent-dependent changes that require legal/ethics review before they can proceed. Generalized from: Metrics instrumentation requires /approve-metrics approval with timeout-triggered escalation

pattern: Do not trust the AC self-audit's '0 unmet/blocked' result as proof of completion — cross-check against the actual criteria-fix gate.
detail: In this run, the self-audit repeatedly reported 8-9/9 met with 0 unmet immediately before the independent criteria-fix check found 1 unmet remaining, across 4+ cycles. The self-audit and the real AC gate can diverge, so treat the self-audit as advisory only.
safety_reason: Requires cross-checking a self-audit against an independent verification gate rather than trusting self-reported completion, adding a verification step.

pattern: Functional blocker (missing schema/entity) ≠ Control flow blocker (planning loop cannot converge)
detail: Functional blockers (e.g., missing users table) require upstream work and show in implementation failure. Control flow blockers (same-prompt loop, insufficient context) require tuning the prompt/planner logic, not merging upstream PRs. Confusing them leads to misdirected escalation.
safety_reason: Teaches proper blocker triage discipline and PROHIBITS misdirected escalation (treating control flow blockers as functional blockers requiring upstream work), which is a verification/methodology check

pattern: When a rendered page is blank but component code exists and DOM root is present, prioritize debugging runtime errors (JS console, unhandled API failures, routing/mount failures) over code structure—wh
detail: Blank visual output paired with well-defined React components and a valid root element indicates a runtime crash or mounting failure, not an architecture problem. Check browser console for unhandled errors, API failures on page load, or import/dependency issues before reviewing component logic.
author: prmr9
safety_reason: Lesson requires runtime error verification (browser console, API failures) before architectural changes—adds a necessary diagnostic check and prohibits premature code refactoring when the actual root

pattern: Tool coverage boundaries don't create security gaps when complementary security layers cover the gap and run in the deployment gate.
detail: Watch for: Generated/compiled artifacts skipped by patch-based SAST (lockfiles, minified code, build outputs) without explicit secondary verification tool in pipeline; Large structured data files that undergo wholesale regeneration (manifests, configs, dependency declarations) where individual diff review is impractical; Automated file changes where human patch review adds no value (regenerated hashes, tool-reordered arrays, formatter-rewritten JSON) marked as security-skipped without documented alternative scanner; Multi-tool security stacks where primary tool (SAST) has known blind spots but no mandatory gate requiring the designed-for secondary tool (npm-audit, container scanners, policy checkers) to execute before merge. Generalized from: Package-lock.json mutations from dependency changes are normal and not scannable by patch-based SAST; rely on npm-audit runner instead.
